name: Hassium
name_origin: Named after the Latin name Hassia for the German state of Hesse, where the GSI laboratory in Darmstadt is located.

discovery.discoverer: Gesellschaft fur Schwerionenforschung (GSI) team led by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Munzenberg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Hassium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators, typically via the fusion reaction 26Mg + 248Cm or 58Fe + 208Pb.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

compounds.name: Hassium tetroxide
compounds.summary: A highly volatile tetroxide identified in gas-phase chromatography experiments. Its formation and adsorption behavior closely parallel OsO4 and confirm hassium's group 8 chemistry.

reactivity_summary: Hassium behaves as a group 8 transition metal, chemically similar to osmium and ruthenium, with a dominant +8 oxidation state. The volatile tetroxide HsO4 is the most thoroughly studied compound and demonstrates osmium-like chemistry consistent with periodic-trend predictions.
isotopes_summary: All hassium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 269Hs (or 270Hs in some assessments) with a half-life on the order of seconds to tens of seconds; this short half-life is sufficient for single-atom gas-phase chemistry.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Hassium
Dullmann, Ch.E. et al. 'Chemical investigation of hassium (element 108)', Nature, 2002
